    20ŌåÆ# Erd┼æs Problem 735
    21ŌåÆ
    22ŌåÆ*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/735](https://www.erdosproblems.com/735)
    23ŌåÆ
    24ŌåÆGiven any $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, when can one assign positive weights to the points
    25ŌåÆsuch that the sum of the weights along every line containing at least two points
    26ŌåÆis the same?
    27ŌåÆ
    28ŌåÆA problem of Murty, who conjectured this is only possible in one of four cases:
    29ŌåÆ1. All points on a line
    30ŌåÆ2. No three points on a line (general position)
    31ŌåÆ3. $n - 1$ points on a line (with one point off)
    32ŌåÆ4. The 7-point incenter configuration: a triangle, the feet of the three angle
    33ŌåÆ   bisectors, and the incenter (or a projective equivalence)
    34ŌåÆ
    35ŌåÆProved by Ackerman, Buchin, Knauer, Pinchasi, and Rote [ABKPR08].
    36ŌåÆ
    37ŌåÆ[ABKPR08] Ackerman, E., Buchin, K., Knauer, C., Pinchasi, R., and Rote, G.,
    38ŌåÆ_There are not too many magic configurations_, Discrete & Computational Geometry 39 (2008), 3--16.
    39ŌåÆ-/

    42ŌåÆ
    43ŌåÆnamespace Erdos735
    44ŌåÆ
    45ŌåÆ/-- The Euclidean plane. -/
    46ŌåÆabbrev R2 := EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2)

    48ŌåÆ/-- A finite set of points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ admits a balanced weighting if there exist positive
    49ŌåÆreal weights such that the total weight on every line through $\geq 2$ points is the same. -/
    50ŌåÆnoncomputable def AdmitsBalancedWeighting (S : Finset R2) : Prop :=
    51ŌåÆ  Ōłā (w : R2 ŌåÆ ŌäØ),
    52ŌåÆ    (ŌłĆ p Ōłł S, w p > 0) Ōł¦
    53ŌåÆ    ŌłĆ pŌéü Ōłł S, ŌłĆ qŌéü Ōłł S, ŌłĆ pŌéé Ōłł S, ŌłĆ qŌéé Ōłł S,
    54ŌåÆ      pŌéü ŌēĀ qŌéü ŌåÆ pŌéé ŌēĀ qŌéé ŌåÆ
    55ŌåÆ      Ōłæ x Ōłł S.filter (fun x => Collinear ŌäØ ({pŌéü, qŌéü, x} : Set R2)), w x =
    56ŌåÆ      Ōłæ x Ōłł S.filter (fun x => Collinear ŌäØ ({pŌéé, qŌéé, x} : Set R2)), w x
    57ŌåÆ

    60ŌåÆ  Collinear ŌäØ (ŌåæS : Set R2)
    61ŌåÆ
    62ŌåÆ/-- No three points of $S$ are collinear ($S$ is in general position). -/
    63ŌåÆdef NoThreeCollinear (S : Finset R2) : Prop :=
    64ŌåÆ  ŌłĆ p Ōłł S, ŌłĆ q Ōłł S, ŌłĆ r Ōłł S,

    71ŌåÆ
    72ŌåÆ/-- The 7-point incenter configuration: vertices $A$, $B$, $C$ of a non-degenerate triangle,
    73ŌåÆthe incenter, and the three feet of the angle bisectors from $A$, $B$, $C$ to the
    74ŌåÆopposite sides. Here $a = |BC|$, $b = |CA|$, $c = |AB|$. The foot from $A$ to $BC$
    75ŌåÆdivides $BC$ in the ratio $c : b$ (by the angle bisector theorem), and similarly
    76ŌåÆfor the other feet. The full classification includes projective equivalences. -/

    19ŌåÆ/-!
    20ŌåÆ# Erd┼æs Problem 210
    21ŌåÆ
    22ŌåÆLet $f(n)$ denote the minimum number of ordinary lines determined by $n$
    23ŌåÆnon-collinear points in the plane. Erd┼æs asked whether $f(n) \to \infty$ and
    24ŌåÆhow fast it grows.
    25ŌåÆ
    26ŌåÆ*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/210](https://www.erdosproblems.com/210)
    27ŌåÆ-/
    28ŌåÆ
    29ŌåÆopen Classical
    30ŌåÆ
    31ŌåÆnamespace Erdos210
    32ŌåÆ
    33ŌåÆ/-- Points in the Euclidean plane $\mathbb{R}^2$. -/
    34ŌåÆabbrev Point2 := EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2)
    35ŌåÆ
    36ŌåÆ/-- Point $r$ lies on the line through distinct points $p$ and $q$. -/
    37ŌåÆdef LiesOnLine (p q r : Point2) : Prop :=
    38ŌåÆ  Ōłā t : ŌäØ, r - p = t ŌĆó (q - p)
    39ŌåÆ
    40ŌåÆ/-- A finite set of points is not all collinear: there exist three
    41ŌåÆnon-collinear points in $S$. -/
    42ŌåÆdef NotAllCollinear (S : Finset Point2) : Prop :=
    43ŌåÆ  Ōłā p Ōłł S, Ōłā q Ōłł S, Ōłā r Ōłł S, p ŌēĀ q Ōł¦ p ŌēĀ r Ōł¦ q ŌēĀ r Ōł¦ ┬¼LiesOnLine p q r
    44ŌåÆ
    45ŌåÆ/-- An ordered pair $(p, q)$ determines an ordinary line for $S$ if $p \neq q$,
    46ŌåÆboth lie in $S$, and no third point of $S$ lies on the line through $p$ and $q$. -/
    47ŌåÆdef IsOrdinaryPair (S : Finset Point2) (p q : Point2) : Prop :=
    48ŌåÆ  p Ōłł S Ōł¦ q Ōłł S Ōł¦ p ŌēĀ q Ōł¦ ŌłĆ r Ōłł S, r ŌēĀ p ŌåÆ r ŌēĀ q ŌåÆ ┬¼LiesOnLine p q r
    49ŌåÆ
    50ŌåÆ/-- The number of ordinary lines determined by $S$.
    51ŌåÆCounts ordered pairs forming ordinary lines and divides by $2$. -/
    52ŌåÆnoncomputable def ordinaryLineCount (S : Finset Point2) : ŌäĢ :=
    53ŌåÆ  ((S ├Ś╦ó S).filter (fun pq => IsOrdinaryPair S pq.1 pq.2)).card / 2
    54ŌåÆ
    55ŌåÆ/--
    56ŌåÆLet $f(n)$ be minimal such that for any $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, not all on a line,
    57ŌåÆthere are at least $f(n)$ ordinary lines (lines containing exactly $2$ of the
    58ŌåÆpoints). Green and Tao proved that $f(n) \geq n/2$ for all sufficiently large $n$,
    59ŌåÆresolving the conjecture of Erd┼æs and de Bruijn and strengthening earlier
    60ŌåÆresults of Motzkin ($f(n) \to \infty$), Kelly-Moser ($f(n) \geq 3n/7$), and
    61ŌåÆCsima-Sawyer ($f(n) \geq 6n/13$).
    62ŌåÆ-/
    63ŌåÆ@[category research solved, AMS 5 52]
    64ŌåÆtheorem erdos_210 :
    65ŌåÆ    Ōłā NŌéĆ : ŌäĢ, ŌłĆ (S : Finset Point2),
    66ŌåÆ      S.card Ōēź NŌéĆ ŌåÆ
    67ŌåÆ      NotAllCollinear S ŌåÆ
    68ŌåÆ      ordinaryLineCount S Ōēź S.card / 2 := by
    69ŌåÆ  sorry
    70ŌåÆ
    71ŌåÆend Erdos210

    19ŌåÆ/-!
    20ŌåÆ# Erd┼æs Problem 506
    21ŌåÆ
    22ŌåÆ*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/506](https://www.erdosproblems.com/506)
    23ŌåÆ
    24ŌåÆWhat is the minimum number of circles determined by $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$,
    25ŌåÆnot all on a circle and not all on a line?
    26ŌåÆ
    27ŌåÆ[Er61] Erd┼æs, P., _Some unsolved problems_, 1961, p. 245.
    28ŌåÆ
    29ŌåÆ[El67] Elliott, P. D. T. A., _On the number of circles determined by $n$ points_, 1967.
    30ŌåÆ
    31ŌåÆ[PuSm] Purdy, G. and Smith, J. W., _Lines, circles, and the number of determined circles_.
    32ŌåÆ-/
    33ŌåÆ
    34ŌåÆopen Finset
    35ŌåÆ
    36ŌåÆnamespace Erdos506
    37ŌåÆ
    38ŌåÆ/-- All points in $S$ lie on a common circle with positive radius. -/
    39ŌåÆnoncomputable def AllOnCircle (S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    40ŌåÆ  Ōłā c : EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2), Ōłā r : ŌäØ, 0 < r Ōł¦ ŌłĆ p Ōłł S, dist p c = r
    41ŌåÆ
    42ŌåÆ/-- All points in $S$ are collinear: they lie on a common affine line. -/
    43ŌåÆnoncomputable def AllCollinear (S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    44ŌåÆ  Ōłā a b : EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2), a ŌēĀ b Ōł¦
    45ŌåÆ    ŌłĆ p Ōłł S, Ōłā t : ŌäØ, p = a + t ŌĆó (b - a)
    46ŌåÆ
    47ŌåÆ/-- The number of distinct circles determined by $S$. A circle is identified by its
    48ŌåÆcenter and positive radius. It is "determined" by $S$ if at least $3$ points of $S$
    49ŌåÆlie on it. -/
    50ŌåÆnoncomputable def numDeterminedCircles (S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2))) : ŌäĢ :=
    51ŌåÆ  Set.ncard {p : EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2) ├Ś ŌäØ |
    52ŌåÆ    0 < p.2 Ōł¦ 3 Ōēż Set.ncard {q Ōłł (ŌåæS : Set (EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2))) | dist q p.1 = p.2}}
    53ŌåÆ
    54ŌåÆ/-- The minimum number of circles determined by any configuration of $n$ points in
    55ŌåÆ$\mathbb{R}^2$ that are neither all on a circle nor all collinear. Returns $0$ when
    56ŌåÆno such configuration exists. -/
    57ŌåÆnoncomputable def minDeterminedCircles (n : ŌäĢ) : ŌäĢ :=
    58ŌåÆ  sInf (numDeterminedCircles '' {S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2)) |
    59ŌåÆ    S.card = n Ōł¦ ┬¼AllOnCircle S Ōł¦ ┬¼AllCollinear S})
    60ŌåÆ
    61ŌåÆ/--
    62ŌåÆErd┼æs Problem 506 [Er61, p. 245]:
    63ŌåÆ
    64ŌåÆWhat is the minimum number of circles determined by $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, not all on a
    65ŌåÆcircle (and not all on a line)?
    66ŌåÆ
    67ŌåÆElliott [El67] proved that for $n > 393$ points not all on a circle or a line, the
    68ŌåÆpoints determine at least $\binom{n-1}{2}$ distinct circles. Purdy and Smith [PuSm]
    69ŌåÆcorrected this to the sharper bound $\binom{n-1}{2} + 1 - \lfloor(n-1)/2\rfloor$, which is best
    70ŌåÆpossible (witnessed by $n-1$ points on a circle and one point off it).
    71ŌåÆ-/
    72ŌåÆ@[category research solved, AMS 52]
    73ŌåÆtheorem erdos_506 : ŌłĆ n : ŌäĢ, 393 < n ŌåÆ
    74ŌåÆ    minDeterminedCircles n =
    75ŌåÆ      answer(Nat.choose (n - 1) 2 + 1 - (n - 1) / 2) := by
    76ŌåÆ  sorry
    77ŌåÆ
    78ŌåÆend Erdos506
    79ŌåÆ

   125ŌåÆ    ┬Ę rw [ŌåÉ EuclideanGeometry.oangle_rotate_sign]
   126ŌåÆ      exact h3

   130ŌåÆtheorem isCcwConvexPolygon_four (A B C D : P) :
   131ŌåÆ    IsCcwConvexPolygon ![A, B, C, D] Ōåö
   132ŌåÆ      (ŌłĪ A B C).sign = 1 Ōł¦ (ŌłĪ B C D).sign = 1 Ōł¦ (ŌłĪ C D A).sign = 1 Ōł¦ (ŌłĪ D A B).sign = 1 :=

   135ŌåÆ/-- The statement that a sequence of points form a convex polygon. -/

   138ŌåÆ
   139ŌåÆ/-- Three affine independent points always form a convex polygon. -/
   140ŌåÆtheorem isConvexPolygon_three_of_affineIndependent {A B C : P}
   141ŌåÆ    (hABC : AffineIndependent ŌäØ ![A, B, C]) : IsConvexPolygon ![A, B, C] := by
   142ŌåÆ  rw [ŌåÉ oangle_ne_zero_and_ne_pi_iff_affineIndependent, ŌåÉ Real.Angle.sign_ne_zero_iff] at hABC
   143ŌåÆ  cases hsABC : (ŌłĪ A B C).sign
   144ŌåÆ  ┬Ę exact (hABC hsABC).elim
   145ŌåÆ  ┬Ę right
   146ŌåÆ    intro i j k hij hjk
   147ŌåÆ    fin_cases i <;> fin_cases j <;> fin_cases k <;> simp at hij hjk
   148ŌåÆ    rw [EuclideanGeometry.oangle_rev, Real.Angle.sign_neg, neg_eq_iff_eq_neg]
   149ŌåÆ    exact (oangle_rotate_sign A B C).trans hsABC
   150ŌåÆ  ┬Ę left
   151ŌåÆ    intro i j k hij hjk
   152ŌåÆ    fin_cases i <;> fin_cases j <;> fin_cases k <;> simp at hij hjk
   153ŌåÆ    exact hsABC
   154ŌåÆ
   155ŌåÆtheorem isConvexPolygon_three_iff_affineIndependent {A B C : P} :
   156ŌåÆ    IsConvexPolygon ![A, B, C] Ōåö AffineIndependent ŌäØ ![A, B, C] := by
   157ŌåÆ  refine Ō¤©fun h => ?_, isConvexPolygon_three_of_affineIndependentŌ¤®
   158ŌåÆ  rw [ŌåÉ oangle_ne_zero_and_ne_pi_iff_affineIndependent, ŌåÉ Real.Angle.sign_ne_zero_iff]
   159ŌåÆ  let p := ![A, B, C]

   161ŌåÆ  change Real.Angle.sign (ŌłĪ (p 0) (p 1) (p 2)) ŌēĀ 0
   162ŌåÆ  cases h with
   163ŌåÆ  | inl h =>
   164ŌåÆ    rw [h.sign_oangle (by simp) (by simp)]
   165ŌåÆ    rintro Ō¤©Ō¤®
   166ŌåÆ  | inr h =>
   167ŌåÆ    suffices Real.Angle.sign (ŌłĪ (p 0) (p 2) (p 1)) = 1 by rw [ŌåÉ oangle_swapŌééŌéā_sign, this]; rintro Ō¤©Ō¤®
   168ŌåÆ    exact h.sign_oangle (i := 0) (j := 1) (k := 2) (by simp) (by simp)
   169ŌåÆ
   170ŌåÆtheorem isConvexPolygon_triangle (t : Affine.Triangle ŌäØ P) : IsConvexPolygon t.points := by
   171ŌåÆ  have : t.points = ![t.points 0, t.points 1, t.points 2] := by ext i; fin_cases i <;> rfl
   172ŌåÆ  rw [this, isConvexPolygon_three_iff_affineIndependent, ŌåÉ this]
   173ŌåÆ  exact t.independent
   174ŌåÆ
   175ŌåÆnoncomputable def triangle_area (a b c : P) : ŌäØ :=
   176ŌåÆ  positiveOrientation.areaForm (a -ßĄź c) (b -ßĄź c) / 2
   177ŌåÆ
   178ŌåÆlemma triangle_area_eq_det (a b c : ŌäØ┬▓) :
   179ŌåÆ    triangle_area a b c =
   180ŌåÆ    Matrix.det !![a 0, b 0, c 0;
   181ŌåÆ                  a 1, b 1, c 1;
   182ŌåÆ                  1,   1,   1] / 2 := by
   183ŌåÆ  rw [triangle_area, Orientation.areaForm_to_volumeForm,
   184ŌåÆ    positiveOrientation.volumeForm_robust (EuclideanSpace.basisFun (Fin 2) ŌäØ) rfl, Module.Basis.det_apply]
   185ŌåÆ  suffices (a 0 - c 0) * (b 1 - c 1) - (b 0 - c 0) * (a 1 - c 1) =
   186ŌåÆ      a 0 * b 1 - a 0 * c 1 - b 0 * a 1 + b 0 * c 1 + c 0 * a 1 - c 0 * b 1 by
   187ŌåÆ    simp [Matrix.det_fin_two, Matrix.det_fin_three, Module.Basis.toMatrix, this]
   188ŌåÆ  ring
   189ŌåÆ
   190ŌåÆ/--
   191ŌåÆGiven a finite set of points in the plane, we define the number of distinct distances between pairs
   192ŌåÆof points.
   193ŌåÆ-/
   194ŌåÆnoncomputable def distinctDistances (points : Finset ŌäØ┬▓) : ŌäĢ :=
   195ŌåÆ  (points.offDiag.image fun (pair : ŌäØ┬▓ ├Ś ŌäØ┬▓) => dist pair.1 pair.2).card
   196ŌåÆ
   197ŌåÆend EuclideanGeometry

   200ŌåÆdef IsIsosceles {╬▒ : Type*} [Dist ╬▒] (p q r : ╬▒) : Prop :=
   201ŌåÆ  dist p q = dist q r Ōł© dist q r = dist r p Ōł© dist r p = dist p q

   204ŌåÆ  Nonempty A Ōł¦ A.Triplewise (IsIsosceles ┬Ę ┬Ę ┬Ę)

    20ŌåÆ# Erd┼æs Problem 217
    21ŌåÆ
    22ŌåÆ*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/217](https://www.erdosproblems.com/217)
    23ŌåÆ
    24ŌåÆFor which $n$ are there $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, no three on a line and no four on a
    25ŌåÆcircle, which determine $n-1$ distinct distances and so that (in some ordering
    26ŌåÆof the distances) the $i$-th distance occurs $i$ times?
    27ŌåÆ
    28ŌåÆAn example with $n=4$ is an isosceles triangle with the point in the centre.
    29ŌåÆErd┼æs originally believed this was impossible for $n \geq 5$, but Pomerance
    30ŌåÆconstructed a set with $n = 5$, and Pal├Īsti proved such sets exist for all $n \leq 8$.
    31ŌåÆErd┼æs believed this is impossible for all sufficiently large $n$.
    32ŌåÆ
    33ŌåÆ[Er83c] [Er87b] [Er97e]
    34ŌåÆ-/
    35ŌåÆ
    36ŌåÆnamespace Erdos217
    37ŌåÆ
    38ŌåÆ/-- Three points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ are collinear if the cross product of $(q - p)$ and $(r - p)$
    39ŌåÆvanishes, i.e. the signed area of the triangle is zero. -/
    40ŌåÆdef Collinear3 (p q r : EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2)) : Prop :=
    41ŌåÆ  (q 0 - p 0) * (r 1 - p 1) = (q 1 - p 1) * (r 0 - p 0)
    42ŌåÆ
    43ŌåÆ/-- A finite point set in $\mathbb{R}^2$ has no three collinear points. -/
    44ŌåÆdef NoThreeCollinear (S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    45ŌåÆ  ŌłĆ p Ōłł S, ŌłĆ q Ōłł S, ŌłĆ r Ōłł S, p ŌēĀ q ŌåÆ p ŌēĀ r ŌåÆ q ŌēĀ r ŌåÆ ┬¼Collinear3 p q r
    46ŌåÆ
    47ŌåÆ/-- Four points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ are concyclic if they are all equidistant from some center. -/
    48ŌåÆdef Concyclic4 (pŌéü pŌéé pŌéā pŌéä : EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2)) : Prop :=
    49ŌåÆ  Ōłā (c : EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2)) (r : ŌäØ),
    50ŌåÆ    dist pŌéü c = r Ōł¦ dist pŌéé c = r Ōł¦ dist pŌéā c = r Ōł¦ dist pŌéä c = r
    51ŌåÆ
    52ŌåÆ/-- A finite point set in $\mathbb{R}^2$ has no four concyclic points. -/
    53ŌåÆdef NoFourConcyclic (S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=

    55ŌåÆ    pŌéü ŌēĀ pŌéé ŌåÆ pŌéü ŌēĀ pŌéā ŌåÆ pŌéü ŌēĀ pŌéä ŌåÆ pŌéé ŌēĀ pŌéā ŌåÆ pŌéé ŌēĀ pŌéä ŌåÆ pŌéā ŌēĀ pŌéä ŌåÆ
    56ŌåÆ    ┬¼Concyclic4 pŌéü pŌéé pŌéā pŌéä
    57ŌåÆ
    58ŌåÆ/-- A set $S$ of $n$ points has the distance multiplicity property if there exist $n-1$
    59ŌåÆdistinct pairwise distances such that (in some ordering) the $j$-th distance
    60ŌåÆoccurs exactly $j$ times as an unordered pair. We count ordered pairs from
    61ŌåÆ`S.offDiag`, so the $j$-th distance yields $2(j+1)$ ordered pairs (since
    62ŌåÆ`Fin (n-1)` is 0-indexed). -/
    63ŌåÆdef HasDistanceMultiplicityProperty (S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    64ŌåÆ  Ōłā (d : Fin (S.card - 1) ŌåÆ ŌäØ),
    65ŌåÆ    Function.Injective d Ōł¦
    66ŌåÆ    (ŌłĆ p Ōłł S, ŌłĆ q Ōłł S, p ŌēĀ q ŌåÆ Ōłā i, dist p q = d i) Ōł¦
    67ŌåÆ    (ŌłĆ i : Fin (S.card - 1),

    72ŌåÆ

    74ŌåÆno three on a line and no four on a circle, which determine $n-1$ distinct

    19ŌåÆ/-!
    20ŌåÆ# Erd┼æs Problem 831
    21ŌåÆ
    22ŌåÆEstimate the function $h(n)$, where $h(n)$ is the minimum number of distinct circumradii
    23ŌåÆdetermined by $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ in general position (no three collinear, no four

    29ŌåÆnamespace Erdos831
    30ŌåÆ
    31ŌåÆ/--
    32ŌåÆA finite point set in $\mathbb{R}^2$ has no three collinear if every three-element subset
    33ŌåÆis not collinear (i.e., no line contains three or more of the points).
    34ŌåÆ-/
    35ŌåÆdef NoThreeCollinear (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    36ŌåÆ  ŌłĆ S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2)),
    37ŌåÆ    S ŌŖå P ŌåÆ S.card = 3 ŌåÆ ┬¼Collinear ŌäØ (S : Set (EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2)))
    38ŌåÆ
    39ŌåÆ/--
    40ŌåÆFour points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ are concyclic if they all lie on a common circle, i.e.,
    41ŌåÆthere exists a center and positive radius such that all four points are
    42ŌåÆequidistant from the center.
    43ŌåÆ-/
    44ŌåÆdef FourPointsConcyclic (S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    45ŌåÆ  Ōłā c : EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2), Ōłā r : ŌäØ, r > 0 Ōł¦ ŌłĆ p Ōłł S, dist p c = r
    46ŌåÆ
    47ŌåÆ/--
    48ŌåÆA finite point set in $\mathbb{R}^2$ has no four concyclic if every four-element subset
    49ŌåÆdoes not lie on a common circle.
    50ŌåÆ-/
    51ŌåÆdef NoFourConcyclic (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    52ŌåÆ  ŌłĆ S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2)),
    53ŌåÆ    S ŌŖå P ŌåÆ S.card = 4 ŌåÆ ┬¼FourPointsConcyclic S
    54ŌåÆ
    55ŌåÆ/--
    56ŌåÆThe number of distinct circumradii of circles passing through three points of $P$.
    57ŌåÆA radius $r$ is counted if there exist three distinct points $a, b, c \in P$ and a
    58ŌåÆcenter $o$ such that all three points lie on the circle of radius $r$ centered at $o$.
    59ŌåÆ-/
    60ŌåÆnoncomputable def distinctCircumradiiCount (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2))) : ŌäĢ :=
    61ŌåÆ  Set.ncard {r : ŌäØ | r > 0 Ōł¦ Ōłā a Ōłł P, Ōłā b Ōłł P, Ōłā c Ōłł P,
    62ŌåÆ    a ŌēĀ b Ōł¦ b ŌēĀ c Ōł¦ a ŌēĀ c Ōł¦
    63ŌåÆ    Ōłā o : EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2), dist a o = r Ōł¦ dist b o = r Ōł¦ dist c o = r}
    64ŌåÆ
    65ŌåÆ/--
    66ŌåÆLet $h(n)$ be maximal such that in any $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ (with no three on a line

    69ŌåÆ
    70ŌåÆFormalized as: $h(n) \to \infty$, i.e., for every $C$ there exists $N$ such that for all
    71ŌåÆ$n \geq N$ and every set $P$ of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ in general position (no three collinear,
    72ŌåÆno four concyclic), the number of distinct circumradii of circles through three
    73ŌåÆpoints is at least $C$.
    74ŌåÆ-/
    75ŌåÆ@[category research open, AMS 5 52]

    77ŌåÆ  ŌłĆ C : ŌäĢ,
    78ŌåÆ    Ōłā N : ŌäĢ, ŌłĆ n : ŌäĢ, n Ōēź N ŌåÆ
    79ŌåÆ      ŌłĆ P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2)),
    80ŌåÆ        P.card = n ŌåÆ
    81ŌåÆ        NoThreeCollinear P ŌåÆ
    82ŌåÆ        NoFourConcyclic P ŌåÆ
    83ŌåÆ        distinctCircumradiiCount P Ōēź C := by
    84ŌåÆ  sorry
    85ŌåÆ

     3ŌåÆ## 1. Code Reuse
     4ŌåÆ
     5ŌåÆ**Collinearity:** Problem 217 defines a custom `Collinear3` using the cross-product formula (line 40ŌĆō41) and wraps it with `NoThreeCollinear` (line 44ŌĆō45). Mathlib provides a canonical `Collinear ŌäØ` definition (in `Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.AffineSpace.FiniteDimensional`), and many other Erd┼æs problems in this codebase already use it:
     6ŌåÆ
     7ŌåÆ- Problem 98 (`98.lean:34ŌĆō36`): `NoThreeCollinear` via Mathlib `Collinear` on 3-element subsets
     8ŌåÆ- Problem 101 (`101.lean:34ŌĆō36`): `NoFiveCollinear` via Mathlib `Collinear`

    13ŌåÆ
    14ŌåÆThe custom `Collinear3` is mathematically equivalent to Mathlib's `Collinear` for three points in ŌäØ┬▓, but using Mathlib's version would improve consistency and eliminate a bespoke definition. Problem 98's `NoThreeCollinear` is directly reusable.
    15ŌåÆ
    16ŌåÆ**Concyclicity:** Problem 217 defines `Concyclic4` (line 48ŌĆō50) without requiring `r > 0`. Other problems in the codebase (98, 130, 831) include the `r > 0` constraint. While this difference is inconsequential for distinct points (see ┬¦6), adopting a shared definition would improve consistency. Problem 98's `FourPointsConcyclic` and `NoFourConcyclic` (lines 43ŌĆō52) could be reused directly.
    17ŌåÆ
    18ŌåÆ**Distance utilities:** The `HasDistanceMultiplicityProperty` is highly specific to this problem and cannot be replaced by generic distance-counting utilities. This is appropriate.
    19ŌåÆ
    20ŌåÆ**Recommendation:** Replace `Collinear3`/`NoThreeCollinear` with Mathlib's `Collinear` (matching the pattern in Problem 98), and consider reusing Problem 98's concyclicity definitions. Alternatively, shared definitions could be factored into a utility file.
    21ŌåÆ
    22ŌåÆ## 2. Citations
    23ŌåÆ
    24ŌåÆThe website lists:
    25ŌåÆ- **[Er83c]**
    26ŌåÆ- **[Er87b, p.167]**
    27ŌåÆ- **[Er97e]**
    28ŌåÆ
    29ŌåÆThe formalization's docstring (line 33) has `[Er83c] [Er87b] [Er97e]`, and the theorem docstring (line 71) has `[Er83c, Er87b, Er97e]`. The page number **p.167** for [Er87b] is missing from both locations. This should be corrected to `[Er87b, p.167]`.
    30ŌåÆ
    31ŌåÆ## 3. Variants
    32ŌåÆ
    33ŌåÆThe formalization captures the main conjecture: for sufficiently large $n$, no such configuration exists. However, two aspects from the website are not captured:
    34ŌåÆ
    35ŌåÆ1. **Known positive results:** Pal├Īsti proved such configurations exist for all $n \leq 8$. This could be formalized as a separate theorem asserting existence for small $n$, which would complement the main conjecture.
    36ŌåÆ
    37ŌåÆ2. **Connection to Problem 98:** The website states that the conjecture "would follow if $h(n) \geq n$ for sufficiently large $n$" where $h(n)$ is the function from Problem 98. This logical implication could be stated as a separate theorem linking the two problems: if $h(n)/n \to \infty$ (Problem 98), then Problem 217 holds. This connection is mathematically substantive ŌĆö any configuration satisfying the distance multiplicity property determines exactly $n - 1$ distinct distances from $\binom{n}{2}$ pairs, which means $h(n) \leq n - 1$, so $h(n) \geq n$ would yield a contradiction.
    38ŌåÆ
    39ŌåÆ3. **The "for which $n$" framing:** The original problem asks "for which $n$" do such configurations exist, which is broader than just the conjecture that they don't exist for large $n$. The formalization only captures one direction.
    40ŌåÆ
    41ŌåÆ## 4. Readability
    42ŌåÆ
    43ŌåÆThe code is generally well-structured and readable. Minor suggestions:
    44ŌåÆ
    45ŌåÆ- The docstring for `HasDistanceMultiplicityProperty` (lines 58ŌĆō62) explains the `2(j+1)` convention for ordered pairs, which is helpful. However, the variable name `d` for the distance function is terse; `dist_vals` or `distances` would be more descriptive.
    46ŌåÆ- The module docstring (lines 19ŌĆō34) is well-written and provides good mathematical context.
    47ŌåÆ- The `NoFourConcyclic` definition quantifies over all ordered 4-tuples with 6 distinctness conditions (line 54ŌĆō56). Using the subset-based approach from Problem 98 (quantifying over 4-element subsets) would be cleaner and more concise.
    48ŌåÆ
    49ŌåÆ## 5. Formalizability
    50ŌåÆ
    51ŌåÆThe problem is **moderately formalizable** with some inherent ambiguity:
    52ŌåÆ
    53ŌåÆ- **"The $i$-th distance occurs $i$ times"**: The formalization interprets this as: there exist $n-1$ distinct distances that can be labeled $d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_{n-1}$ such that $d_i$ occurs exactly $i$ times as an unordered pair. This is a natural and standard reading.
    54ŌåÆ- **Counting convention**: The formalization uses ordered pairs via `offDiag` and compensates with a factor of 2, which is correct since `dist` is symmetric.
    55ŌåÆ- **"Sufficiently large $n$"**: Formalized as $\exists N_0, \forall n \geq N_0$, which is standard.
    56ŌåÆ- The problem statement is precise enough that the formalization is essentially forced ŌĆö there is minimal ambiguity in how to interpret it.
    57ŌåÆ
    58ŌåÆ**Assessment:** Low ambiguity. The formalization choices are natural.
    59ŌåÆ
    60ŌåÆ## 6. Correctness
    61ŌåÆ
    62ŌåÆThe formalization is **mathematically correct** with one minor observation:
    63ŌåÆ
    64ŌåÆ**`Concyclic4` without `r > 0`:** The definition (line 48ŌĆō50) allows `r = 0`, which would require all four points to coincide at the center `c`. Since `NoFourConcyclic` (line 53ŌĆō56) requires all four points to be pairwise distinct, `r = 0` is impossible in context. Thus the omission of `r > 0` is **harmless** ŌĆö the definitions are equivalent under the distinctness hypotheses. Nevertheless, adding `r > 0` (as in Problems 98, 130, 831) would make the standalone `Concyclic4` definition more mathematically natural and prevent potential misuse outside this context.
    65ŌåÆ
    66ŌåÆ**`HasDistanceMultiplicityProperty` verification:**
    67ŌåÆ- `Fin (S.card - 1)` gives indices $0, 1, \ldots, n-2$ (0-indexed).
    68ŌåÆ- For index $i$, the required count is $2(i+1)$ ordered pairs = $i+1$ unordered pairs.
    69ŌåÆ- Total unordered pairs: $\sum_{i=0}^{n-2} (i+1) = 1 + 2 + \cdots + (n-1) = \binom{n}{2}$. Ō£ō
    70ŌåÆ- Number of distinct distances: $n - 1$ (the cardinality of `Fin (S.card - 1)`). Ō£ō
    71ŌåÆ- The injectivity of `d` ensures all $n-1$ distances are distinct. Ō£ō
    72ŌåÆ- The coverage condition (line 66) ensures every pair has its distance among the $d_i$. Ō£ō
    73ŌåÆ
    74ŌåÆThese are all consistent with the problem statement. The formalization correctly captures the conjecture.
    75ŌåÆ
    76ŌåÆ**`Collinear3` correctness:** The cross-product formula $(q_0 - p_0)(r_1 - p_1) = (q_1 - p_1)(r_0 - p_0)$ correctly characterizes collinearity of three points in ŌäØ┬▓. This is equivalent to requiring the vectors $q - p$ and $r - p$ are linearly dependent, i.e., the $2 \times 2$ determinant vanishes. Ō£ō
    77ŌåÆ
    78ŌåÆ**Main theorem structure:** `erdos_217` asserts $\exists N_0$ such that for all sufficiently large point sets with no three collinear and no four concyclic, the distance multiplicity property fails. This correctly formalizes Erd┼æs's conjecture. Ō£ō
    79ŌåÆ
    80ŌåÆ**Overall assessment:** The formalization is correct and complete for the main conjecture. The primary improvement opportunities are (a) using Mathlib's `Collinear` for consistency with the rest of the codebase, (b) adding the `p.167` page reference, and (c) optionally formalizing the connection to Problem 98 and the known positive results for $n \leq 8$.

1ŌåÆ# Review: Erd┼æs Problem 506
     2ŌåÆ
     3ŌåÆ## 1. Code Reuse
     4ŌåÆ
     5ŌåÆSeveral opportunities for reuse exist:
     6ŌåÆ
     7ŌåÆ- **`AllCollinear`** (line 43): Defined via a custom parametric line representation (`Ōłā a b, a ŌēĀ b Ōł¦ ŌłĆ p Ōłł S, Ōłā t, p = a + t ŌĆó (b - a))`). Erd┼æs 735 (`FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/735.lean:59`) defines the same concept more idiomatically as `Collinear ŌäØ (ŌåæS : Set R2)`, directly using Mathlib's `Collinear`. The Mathlib version is preferableŌĆöit integrates with existing lemmas and is semantically equivalent.
     8ŌåÆ
     9ŌåÆ- **`AllOnCircle`** (line 39): Nearly identical to `FourPointsConcyclic` in Erd┼æs 831 (`FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/831.lean:44`) and Erd┼æs 98/217/130. Both define "all points lie on a circle with positive radius." The only difference is that `AllOnCircle` applies to the full set while `FourPointsConcyclic` is typically applied to subsets of size 4. A shared definition parameterized by cardinality or applied to arbitrary sets would reduce duplication.
    10ŌåÆ
    11ŌåÆ- **`EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2)`**: The library provides the `ŌäØ┬▓` notation in `FormalConjecturesForMathlib/Geometry/2d.lean:25` and a `Point2` abbreviation in Erd┼æs 210 (`FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/210.lean:34`). Problem 506 uses the raw type throughout; adopting one of these conventions would improve consistency.
    12ŌåÆ
    13ŌåÆ- **`numDeterminedCircles`** (line 50): The pattern of counting geometric objects via `Set.ncard` over a set comprehension is shared with `threeRichUnitCircleCount` in Erd┼æs 104 (`FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/104.lean:35`) and `distinctCircumradiiCount` in Erd┼æs 831 (`FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/831.lean:60`). While the predicates differ, the structural pattern is the same.
    14ŌåÆ
    15ŌåÆ## 2. Citations
    16ŌåÆ
    17ŌåÆThe formalization includes:
    18ŌåÆ- `[Er61]` Erd┼æs, P., *Some unsolved problems*, 1961, p. 245 ŌĆö **correct**
    19ŌåÆ- `[El67]` Elliott, P. D. T. A., *On the number of circles determined by n points*, 1967 ŌĆö **correct**
    20ŌåÆ- `[PuSm]` Purdy, G. and Smith, J. W., *Lines, circles, and the number of determined circles* ŌĆö **correct**
    21ŌåÆ
    22ŌåÆ**Missing from the website:**
    23ŌåÆ- `[BaBa94]` B├Īlint, V. and B├Īlintov├Ī, A. ŌĆö The website lists this as an additional reference confirming the result. It should be added.
    24ŌåÆ
    25ŌåÆThe website also notes related problems **#104** and **#831**, which are not cross-referenced in the docstring.
    26ŌåÆ
    27ŌåÆ## 3. Variants
    28ŌåÆ
    29ŌåÆThe formalization captures only the **large n** regime (n > 393), which is the fully resolved case.
    30ŌåÆ
    31ŌåÆ**Missing variants:**
    32ŌåÆ- **Small n (n Ōēż 393):** The website states this remains open. Segre showed that Elliott's original bound `C(n-1, 2)` fails for n = 8 (via projection of a cube onto a plane). The formalization could include a separate conjecture or open problem statement for small n.
    33ŌåÆ- **The problem as originally posed** asks simply "what is the minimum number of circles?" without specifying the answerŌĆöit's a question, not a conjecture. A more faithful formalization might state the lower bound (`Ōēź`) separately from the exact value (`=`), since the lower bound and the matching upper bound (via the witnessing configuration) are logically distinct results.
    34ŌåÆ
    35ŌåÆ## 4. Readability
    36ŌåÆ
    37ŌåÆ- **Good:** The namespace `Erdos506` cleanly scopes all definitions. The docstrings are clear and informative. The `open Finset` is minimal and appropriate.
    38ŌåÆ- **Improvement:** Replace `EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2)` with `ŌäØ┬▓` (from `FormalConjecturesForMathlib/Geometry/2d.lean`) or introduce a local abbreviation like `Point2` (as in Erd┼æs 210) to reduce verbosity.
    39ŌåÆ- **Improvement:** The `AllCollinear` definition using explicit parametric lines is harder to read than the Mathlib-idiomatic `Collinear ŌäØ (ŌåæS : Set ...)`. The parametric form requires the reader to mentally verify equivalence with standard collinearity.
    40ŌåÆ- **Minor:** The `noncomputable` annotation on `AllOnCircle` and `AllCollinear` is technically unnecessary since they are `Prop`-valued (propositions are always computable in Lean 4's type theory). However, this is a style issue and has no semantic impact since the file uses noncomputable defaults elsewhere.
    41ŌåÆ
    42ŌåÆ## 5. Formalizability
    43ŌåÆ
    44ŌåÆ**Assessment: High ŌĆö unambiguous for the large-n case.**
    45ŌåÆ
    46ŌåÆThe problem as stated on the website ("What is the minimum number of circles determined by n points, not all on a circle?") is a well-posed optimization question. The formalization resolves the key ambiguities:
    47ŌåÆ
    48ŌåÆ- **"Determined by"** is formalized as "at least 3 points of S lie on the circle" ŌĆö this is the standard combinatorial-geometry interpretation.
    49ŌåÆ- **"Not all on a circle"** is `┬¼AllOnCircle S`.
    50ŌåÆ- **"Not all on a line"** (`┬¼AllCollinear S`) is an implicit non-degeneracy condition not stated in the original problem but necessary for a meaningful result. Without it, collinear configurations yield 0 determined circles (three collinear points do not lie on any circle), making the infimum trivially 0. The formalization correctly includes this.
    51ŌåÆ
    52ŌåÆThe only remaining ambiguity is for **small n** where the exact minimum is unknown, making that regime inherently not yet formalizable as an exact equality.
    53ŌåÆ
    54ŌåÆ## 6. Correctness
    55ŌåÆ
    56ŌåÆ**Assessment: Correct, with one minor concern.**
    57ŌåÆ
    58ŌåÆ### What is correct:
    59ŌåÆ
    60ŌåÆ- **Circle identification:** A circle in ŌäØ┬▓ is uniquely determined by its center and positive radius. The formalization correctly identifies circles as elements of `EuclideanSpace ŌäØ (Fin 2) ├Ś ŌäØ` with positive second component. No two distinct pairs `(center, radius)` describe the same geometric circle.
    61ŌåÆ
    62ŌåÆ- **The formula:** `Nat.choose (n - 1) 2 + 1 - (n - 1) / 2` correctly encodes `C(n-1, 2) + 1 - ŌīŖ(n-1)/2Ōīŗ`. Since all arithmetic is over `ŌäĢ`:
    63ŌåÆ  - `Nat.choose (n-1) 2 = (n-1)(n-2)/2` ŌĆö correct.
    64ŌåÆ  - `(n-1) / 2` is natural number floor division ŌĆö matches `ŌīŖ(n-1)/2Ōīŗ`.
    65ŌåÆ  - The subtraction is well-defined (no underflow) for n > 393 since `C(n-1,2) + 1` grows quadratically while `(n-1)/2` is linear.
    66ŌåÆ
    67ŌåÆ- **The threshold n > 393:** Matches the literature (Elliott's original bound applies for n > 393, and Purdy-Smith's correction preserves this threshold).
    68ŌåÆ
    69ŌåÆ- **Non-degeneracy conditions:** Both `┬¼AllOnCircle S` and `┬¼AllCollinear S` are necessary and correct. The problem would be trivial without either condition.
    70ŌåÆ
    71ŌåÆ- **`sInf` behavior:** When no valid configuration exists (e.g., for very small n where no set of n points can be non-collinear and non-concyclic), `sInf Ōłģ = 0` for `ŌäĢ`, which matches the docstring's stated behavior ("returns 0 when no such configuration exists").
    72ŌåÆ
    73ŌåÆ- **Equality vs. inequality:** The theorem uses `=` (exact minimum), which is justified: Purdy-Smith proved the lower bound, and the configuration of n-1 points on a circle plus one point off it achieves exactly this count, establishing a matching upper bound.
    74ŌåÆ
    75ŌåÆ### Minor concern:
    76ŌåÆ
    77ŌåÆ- **`minDeterminedCircles` uses `sInf` on a set of natural numbers.** In Mathlib, `sInf` on `ŌäĢ` returns 0 for the empty set and otherwise the true infimum (minimum, since ŌäĢ is well-ordered). For the image set `numDeterminedCircles '' {S | ...}`, if the set is nonempty (which it is for n Ōēź 4, say), `sInf` correctly returns the minimum. This is fine, but worth noting that the definition implicitly relies on the well-ordering of ŌäĢ and the behavior of `sInf` for `ŌäĢ` in Mathlib.
    78ŌåÆ
    79ŌåÆ### Overall verdict:
    80ŌåÆ
    81ŌåÆThe formalization is **mathematically sound** and faithfully captures the resolved (large-n) case of Erd┼æs Problem 506. The main suggestions are stylistic: use Mathlib's `Collinear` instead of the custom parametric definition, adopt the `ŌäØ┬▓` notation for consistency, add the missing `[BaBa94]` citation, and consider cross-referencing related problems #104 and #831.
    82ŌåÆ
